step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Extending path rightward toward green block')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: UP moved the agent from rows 36-41 to rows 30-35 (moved up 6 rows). The agent moves with arrow keys, and the path seems to be a trail that follows the agent. The path tiles from the old position are gone.
- Action model: Arrow keys move the agent. RIGHT also extends a horizontal trail. Need to understand the trail mechanic better.
- Plan: Check current path position, then figure out how to navigate to the colored blocks in the correct order (Red → Green → Blue per bottom panel).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the agent is at rows 30-35, cols 11-16. The path tiles are now at rows 32-33 (the center of the agent's new position). The path extends rightward from the agent.
Looking at the layout:
- Agent: rows 30-35, cols 11-16 (6x6 object with pink border and white center)
- Path extends right from agent at rows 32-33
- Colored blocks on right wall: Red (19-22), Blue (25-28), Green (31-34)
- Bottom panel order: Pink → Red → Green → Blue
The agent is 6 rows tall. When I pressed UP, it moved from rows 36-41 to rows 30-35 (moved up 6 rows). This seems like it moved to align with the green block (rows 31-34).
Wait, let me reconsider. The agent is at rows 30-35, and the green block is at rows 31-34. The agent's vertical center is around row 32-33, which aligns with the green block.
So maybe the mechanic is:
- UP/DOWN moves the agent to align with different colored blocks vertically
- RIGHT extends a path toward the blocks
- The goal is to build paths from the agent to each colored block in the correct order
The bottom panel shows: Pink → Red → Green → Blue
But the pink IS the agent. So maybe the order is: first connect to Red, then Green, then Blue.
Let me try pressing RIGHT multiple times to extend the path all the way to the right wall, and see what happens when it reaches the colored blocks.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the agent is now aligned with the green block (both at roughly rows 31-34). If I extend the path right, it would hit the green block.
But the bottom panel says the order is: Pink → Red → Green → Blue. So I should connect to Red first.
Let me try pressing UP again to align with the Red block (rows 19-22).
Wait, actually let me reconsider. The agent moved from rows 36-41 to rows 30-35 with one UP press. That's a 6-row movement. The agent is 6 rows tall. So each UP/DOWN press moves the agent by 6 rows (one "block height").
To reach Red (rows 19-22), I'd need to move up from rows 30-35. The Red block is at rows 19-22, so I need the agent's center to align there. Currently the agent is at rows 30-35. One more UP would move it to rows 24-29, which would align with Blue (rows 25-28). Two more UPs would reach rows 18-23, aligning with Red (rows 19-22).
Hmm, but let me think about this differently. Maybe I should first try to extend the path to the right and see what happens when it reaches the colored blocks. The current alignment is with the Green block.
Actually, let me re-examine. The bottom panel order is Pink, Red, Green, Blue. The agent is Pink. So maybe the sequence means:
1. Start with Pink (agent) - already done
2. Connect to Red
3. Connect to Green
4. Connect to Blue
I need to navigate to Red first. Let me try UP UP to align with Red, then RIGHT to extend the path.
But wait - the budget says about 61 target actions. That's a lot. Let me think about whether there's a more efficient approach. Maybe I should batch movements.
Let me first try: UP UP RIGHT... to align with Red and extend the path.
Actually, let me first understand what happens when the path reaches a colored block. Let me try extending RIGHT until it hits the green block (since we're already aligned with it).
The path currently extends to about col 25. The colored blocks are at cols 42-45. So I need about 17 more RIGHT presses to reach them. That's a lot of actions.
Let me try a few RIGHTs and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Agent is at rows 30-35, cols 11-16. The path extends rightward from the agent at rows 32-33, currently reaching about col 25. The agent is vertically aligned with the Green block (rows 31-34). Each UP/DOWN moves the agent 6 rows. Colored blocks: Red (19-22), Blue (25-28), Green (31-34), all at cols 42-45.
Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
Plan: Try extending RIGHT to see what happens when the path reaches the green block (current alignment).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try extending RIGHT several times to reach the green block
# The path is at col ~25, blocks are at col 42-45, need ~17 more RIGHTs
# Let's batch them
predict(expect_change=True, note='Extending path rightward toward green block')
action(['RIGHT'] * 5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== Path tiles after 5 RIGHTs ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] <= 4 and n['color'] in ['w', 'g']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = min(rows); c = min(cols)
        if 30 <= r <= 36 and 15 <= c <= 50:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} pos=({r},{c})")

print("\n=== Agent ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"  rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
